text: BENOIT-SUR-LOIRE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, and cant. of Ouzouersur-Loire. It contains a curious church. Pop. 1,540.
BENOIT-DE-SEYSSIEU (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ain, and cant. of Lhuis, 8 m. SW of Belley, near the Rhone. The locality affords good red wines. Pop. 1,221.
BENON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lower Charente, and cant. of Courçon. Pop. 963. In the vicinity of this town is the forest of the same name.

BENOIT (LAKE), a sheet of water in Lower BEN Canada, connected by the Portage of Pelletier bay with the Saguenay river. It is surrounded by mountains; and, although of irregular shape, is of considerable extent.
BENPENSURA, a town of Sicily, in the prov. and 17 m. E of Caltanicetta.

BENRATH, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the circle and 6 m. SE of Dusseldorf, on the r. bank of the Rhine. It contains a royal castle, and possesses some cloth manufactories. The environs are adorned with gardens. Pop. 700.
BENREISIPOL, a mountain in Sunart, in Argyllshire, midway between Loch Shiel and Loch Sunart. Alt. 2,651 ft.
BENSALEM, a town of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Bucks co. Pop. in 1840, 1,731.

BENSBERG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the circle of Muhlheim, 9 m. ENE of Cologne. It contains a fine castle, built by the electors-palatine; and in the environs are quarries of greyish marble. Pop. 740. BENSEN, BENESCHAU, or BENSDORF, a town of Bohemia, the cap. of the seigniory of the same name, on the Pulsnitz, 20 m. NNE of Leitmaritz. It contains castles belonging to the counts of Thun and princes of Clary, and possesses manufactories of coarse linen.
BENSHAUSEN. See BENNSHAUSEN.

BENSHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, the cap. of a bailiwick of the same name, in the prov. of Starkenburg, on a tributary of the Weschnitz, 13 m. S of Darmstadt. It contains a church, a college, a gymnasium, a Catholic normal school, and an alms-house; and holds four annual fairs. The environs afford several kinds of wine. Pop. 3,997.—The bailiwick is divided into the bailiwick of the Grand Duke, and the seignorial bailiwick of Schönberg. Pop. 8,857.
BENSINGTON, or Benson, a parish in Oxfordshire, 2 m. NE of Wallingford. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. 1,254.
BENSON, a township of Vermont, U.S., in Rutland co., 85 m. SW of Montpelier, and 62 m. S of Burlington. It is situated on Lake Champlain, which is here about a mile in width. Pop. in 1840, 1,403.
BENSRODE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 51 m. ENE of Ougein.

BENSTEDT, or BENNSTEDT, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony and circle of Mannesfeld, 6 m. W of Halle. White porcelain clay is found in the environs, and is exported in considerable quantities to Berlin. Pop. 500.
BENTALA, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Futa-Jallon, on a tributary of the Rio Grande, 60 m. WNW of Labe. It is an important commercial entrepot.
BENTFIELD, a hamlet in the p. of Stansted- Mountfichet, Essex. Area 740 acres. Pop. 496.
BENTHALL, a parish in Salop, 2 m. NE of Much-Wenlock. Area 770 acres. Pop. 587.

BENTHAM, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 12 m. WNW of Settle. Area 23,100 acres. Pop. 3,535, of whom 2,180 were in the township of B.— Also a hamlet in the p. of Badgeworth, Gloucestershire. Pop. 236.

BENTHEIM, a county of Germany, belonging formerly to the family of Bentheim-Bentheim, but now included in the kingdom of Hanover. It lies between the bishopric of Munster and the Dutch prov. of Overyssel; and is included in the gov. of Osnabrück. It is about 45 m. long, and from 9 m. to 18 m. broad. Its area is 19 German or 403.5 English sq. m. Pop. in 1833, 34,850, speaking Dutch, and of whom about five-sixths were Calvinists. In 1806 the co. of B. was annexed first to the grandduchy of Berg, and afterwards to the French departments of the Mouths of the Yssel and of the West Ems. At the peace of 1814 it was incorporated with Hanover. Bentheim, a market-town, or rather village, in the kingdom of Hanover, the cap. of the above district, situated on an eminence, 38 m. WNW of Osnabruck. It is the seat of a criminal court, and of a court of appeal. Its chief manufactures are leather and parchment. In the environs are sulphurous springs. The castle or palace of the counts stands here on a high rock, flanked with towers, and was garrisoned for many years by the troops of the bishop of Munster. In 1760, B. was bombarded and taken by the French, but was soon after retaken by the Allies, and part of its works blown up. It was again taken by the French in 1799.
BENTHUIZEN, a town of Holland, in the prov. of South Holland, 8 m. S by E of Leyden.

BENTINCK'S ARMS, two branches of an inlet of the North Pacific, discovered by Vancouver, on the W coast of North America, -one to the NE, the other to the SE, in E long. 233° to 233° 21', N lat. 52° to 52° 25'.
BENTINCK POINT, the NE point of Hinchinbrook island, on the W coast of North America, in E long. 214° 24′, N lat. 60° 28′.

BENTINCK ISLAND, an island of Australia, in the gulf of Carpentaria. It is about 10 m. in extent either way, is slightly elevated, and thickly wooded. Its E end approaches within 13 m. of Point Parker.

BENTLEY, a parish in Hampshire, 4 m. WSW of Farnham. Area 2,450 acres. Pop. 766.—Also a township in the p. of Wolverhampton, Staffordshire, 2 m. N by W of Walsall. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 428.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 6 m. SW of Ipswich. Area 2,350 acres. Pop. 419.—Also a hamlet in Shustock p., Warwickshire, 3 m. SW of Atherstone. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 266.—Also a hamlet in Rowley p., E. R. of Yorkshire. Pop. 62.
BENTLEY-WITH-ARKSEY, a p. in the W. R. of Yorkshire, north division of Strafforth wapentake. Area 5,220 acres. Pop. 1,056.
BENTLEY-FENNY, a parish in Derbyshire, 2 m. N by W of Ashbourne. Area 670 acres. Pop. 343.
BENTLEY (GREAT), a parish in Essex, 8 m. ESE of Colchester. Area 2,850 acres. Pop. 1,005.
BENTLEY (HUNGRY), a liberty in Longford p., Derbyshire. Pop. 83.
BENTLEY (LITTLE), a parish in Essex, 5 m. S by E of Manningtree. Area 2,750 acres. Pop. 462.
BENTLEY-PAUNCEFOOT, a township in the p. of Tardebigg, in Worcestershire. Pop. 3,552.
BENTLEYVILLE, a village in Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 198 m. W of Harrisburg.—Also a v. in Halifax co., Virginia, 115 m. SW of Richmond.

BENTON, a county in the state of Alabama, U.S., on the Coosa and Tallapoosa rivers. Area 1,060 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 14,260.—Also a county in the W of Tennessee, having the Tennessee river for its E boundary. Area 375 sq. m. Pop. 4,772.—Also a county in the SW part of Missouri. Area 1,050 sq. m. Pop. 4,205.—Also a county in Arkansas, in the NW corner of the state, on the White river, and head-branches of the Illinois and Cowskin. Area 1,050 sq. m. Pop. 2,228.—Also a township in Yates co., in the state of New York, 184 m. W of Albany. Pop. 3,911.—Also a village in Lumpkin co., in the state of Georgia, 125 m. NNW of Milledgeville .- Also a village in Holmes co., Ohio, 92 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 150.—Also the cap. of Scott co., in the state of Missouri, 222 m. SE of Jefferson.-Also the cap. of Yazoo co., in the state of Mississippi, 50 m. NW of Jackson. Pop. 400.
BENTON (Long), a parish in Northumberland, BEN 3 m. NE of Newcastle. Area 8,760 acres. Pop. 8,711, of whom 2,451 were in the township of Long B.
BENTON-RIDGE, a village in Hancock co., Ohio, 99 m. N by W of Columbus.

BENTONVILLE, a village, the cap. of Polk co., Tennessee, 174 m. SE of Nashville, on Four-mile branch of Highawassee river. Pop. 200.-Also the cap. of Benton co., Arkansas, 2 m. S of Little Sugar creek. Pop. 134.
BENTOTTE, a village on the SW coast of the island of Ceylon, 18 m. S by E of Caltura, at the mouth of a small river.
BENTSCHEN, or Pozewo, a town of Prussia, in the duchy of Pozen, 20 m. SE of Meseritz. Pop. 1,350.
BENTWISCH, a town of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, NE of Rostock.
BENTWORTH, a parish in Hampshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. W of Alton. Area 3,770 acres. Pop. 609.
BENUB, a village in the delta of Egypt, near Bebayt-el-Hagar, and on the site of the ancient Omphis.
BENUD, a town of Upper Egypt, in the prov. and 8 m. S of Gheneh, on the r. bank of the Nile.
BENUM, a town of Sudan, the cap. of the kingdom of Ludamar, 330 m. WNW of Jenne. It consists of ill-built huts scattered over an extensive area.
BENVENUE, a mountain rising above the E end of Loch Katrine in Perthshire. Its alt. is about 3,000 ft.
BENVORLICH, a mountain in the p. of Arrochar, Dumbartonshire, on the W side of Loch Lomond. It has an alt. of about 3,000 ft.
BENVRACKY, a mountain in Perthshire, which terminates the vale of Athole on the one hand, and the strath of the Garry on the other. Alt. 2,756 ft.
BENWELL, a township in the p. of St. John, Northumberland, 2 m. W of Newcastle-on-Tyne. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. 1,433.
BENWICK, a chapelry in the p. of Doddington, 6 m. SW of March. Area 3,060 acres. Pop. 679.

BENWYVIS, a mountain in the parishes of Kiltearn and Fodderty, near the E coast of Ross-shire. Alt., according to the New Statistical account, 3,426 ft., but according to Mr. Schmidt, 3,722 ft. It is seldom entirely free from snow.
BE'NY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Coligny. Pop. 1,016.
BENY-BOCAGE (LE), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Vire. The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,375. The com., 6 m. N of Vire, has a pop. of 862.
BENYEVENAGH, a mountain in co. Londonderry, 23 m. E of Lough Foyle. Alt. 1,260 ft.

BENZERTA (Lakes of), two lakes in the N part of the regency of Tunis,—the Hipponitus lacus and Sisaræ palus of early geographers. They run in an ENE and WSW direction, and are each about 8 m. in length. The more northern of the two, generally called the lake of B., is 5\frac{1}{2} m. broad; the other, sometimes distinguished as the lake of Jebel Ishkil, is only 31 m. in breadth. The larger lake is clear and salt, communicating with the sea, with a depth of from 5 to 8 fath.; the smaller is a turbid sheet of fresh water, only 8 ft. deep. Both form valuable preserves of fish, whence the town of Tunis is entirely supplied during a great part of the year. A meandering channel, running through a low neck of land about 2 m. broad, connects the two lakes. The lake of B. communicates with the sea by a stream issuing from its NE angle, about 4 m. in length, and a 1 m. broad.—The town of Benzerta lies at its mouth, in a fertile and beautiful district, laid out in olivegroves, vineyards, open pastures, and grain-crops.- Lieut. Spratt.
BEODRA, a town of Austria, in the Bannat, 47 m. W of Temesvar.
BEOLEY, a parish of Worcestershire, 7 m. E of Bromsgrove. Area 4,700 acres. Pop. 657.
BEPTON, a parish in Sussex, 3 m. SW of Midhurst. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 207.
BEQ. See Bic.

BEQUETTE, or BIQUETTE, an island in the St. Lawrence river, in N lat. 48° 25' 20", W long. 68° 54'. It is a ½ m. in length, a ¼ m. broad; and rises about 100 ft. above sea-level. There is a revolving light on its W point, 112 ft. above high water, seen in clear weather at 17 m. distance. It has a dangerous reef 14 m. due W from its W end; and several rocks to the E and SE diminish the channel between it and Bic island to little more than a 1 m. in breadth. The E point of Bequette is 143 m. E of Barnaby island.
BEQUIA. See BEKIA.
BER (Kis), a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 17 m. S of Komorn, on a tributary of the Danube.
BERABZAN, a lake of British N America, in Churchill territory, in N lat. 60°, and W long. 97°.
BERAGH, a village in the p. of Clogherney, in co. Tyrone. Pop. 617.
BERAHRAH, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Hedjaz, 75 m. SE of Mecca, on the route to Sada.

BERAIN-SUR-D'HEUNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Givry, near the Canal-du-Centre. It contains extensive bottle-works; and in the vicinity are mines of coal and gypsum. Pop. 724.
BERAIN-SOUS-SANVIGNES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Montcenis, 19 m. S of Autun. Coal is found in the locality. Pop. 1,170.
BERAITCH. See BARAITCHE.
BERAK, or Burak, a town of Syria, in the pash. and 21 m. SSE of Damascus, near the l. bank of the Lowa.
BERAN, a village of Spain, in Galicia, 20 m. W of Orense. It has thermal springs and baths.

BERAR, a territory of Southern Hindostan, in the Deccan, forming a table-land, or rather an elevated valley, towards the centre of the country, between the parallels of 17° and 23°. It is bounded on the N by the Ceded districts on the Nerbudda; on the E by the prov. of Gundwana; on the S by Bidar and Aurungabad; and on the W by the Nizam's dominions. It is of very irregular outline; and has an area of 56,723 sq. m.; but it was formerly of much greater extent. On the S it is bounded by a chain of mountains, the ghauts or passes leading through which are in many places very difficult. This range attains its greatest alt. near Salah, and on the northern flank, in some places, descends rapidly to the plains of B. On the N its boundary is a high table-land. Its SW frontier is defined by the Wurda river. Its other principal rivers are the Tapti or Tuptee, the Paingunga, and the Mahanuddi. The latter is the only stream available for navigation. Boats drawing not more than 3 ft., may navigate it from Cuttack to Sury-Narain, from the month of September till February. In the rainy season it runs with so much rapidity that boats may make the passage downwards, from Sumbhulpore to Cuttack, a distance of nearly 250 m., in two days, without using either sails or oars. The soil is generally rich, but ill-cultivated. Wheat, Indian corn, grain, pease, vetches, and flax, are cultivated. The wheat of Magpore is reckoned the best in India. Three months suffice to rear a wheat crop, which is usually succeeded in the same year by one of maize. Sugar, betel-leaf, and tobacco are also cultivated, and opium to a small extent. There is little external commerce. The subdivisions of B. are Beytulbari or Baitalbari, Gawil- BER gur, Kullum, Mahore, Maicar, Nagpore, Nernallah, Wanassim or Wasim, and Wynegunga. The chief towns are Amaravati, Assaye, and Ellichpur.-The family of the present rajah of B. first rose into power about the year 1700. In 1799, after the treaty of Basrein, the rajah of B. entered into a league with Sindiah, whereupon the first Mahratta war ensued, which cost the rajah the prov. of Cuttack and the port of Balasore, ceded to the English in 1803. In 1809 B. was invaded by a Patan chief, who was expelled by the assistance of a British force. In 1816, the new rajah, Appah Sahib, joined the second Mahratta confederacy, and was punished for his treachery by being compelled to cede the districts of Sumbhulpur, Patna, and Sirguja, whose rajahs and zemindars had long earnestly desired to place themselves under the protection of the British government. Soon afterwards the rajah, being detected in a third intrigue, was deposed; and a boy was selected from the family to occupy the musnud. During his minority of seven years, the administration of B. was conducted by the English resident. The system of administration introduced by the resident is still adhered to by the rajah; and B. enjoys one of the best native governments in India. The rajah, in consideration of his right to claim protection from the Company's government, pays a tribute of 8 lacs of rupees per ann., and is further bound to supply a body of 1,000 cavalry whenever required.
BERARD, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Loire, and com. of Outrefurens. In the vicinity are extensive coal-mines.
BERARDIERE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Loire, and com. of Valbenoite, nearly 2 m. SW of St. Etienne. It contains important iron and steel-works.

BERASTEGUI, a town of Spain, in Guipuscoa, 13 m. S of St. Sebastian, situated on a height on the frontier of the prov. of Pampeluna. It contains a town-house and a public square. In the adjacent valley of Leizaran are an iron mine and forges.
BERAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Carbonne. Pop. 1,103.

BERA'T, or ARNAUT-BELGRADE, a city of Turkey in Europe, in Albania, in the sanjak and 27 m. NE of Valona, and 92 m. NW of Janini. It is romantically situated in a narrow defile, at the base of the gigantic and lofty Mount Tomaros, Tomerit, or Tomur, on the r. bank of the Beratino or Kavroni, the ancient Apsus, 25 m. above the entrance of that river into the Adriatic. "Along either bank of the winding stream is spread the town; and the two banks are united by a high and handsome bridge." It is defended by a castle built on a height, and reputed the strongest in Albania; and to the NW of the citadel is an eminence surmounted by a small fort flanked with towers. The city contains the palace of the vizier, and about 250 houses; and is the residence of a Greek archbishop. Its commerce is active, especially with Trieste. The pop., consisting chiefly of Greeks and Arnauts, is estimated at from 8,000 to 10,000. Some geographers suppose B. to occupy the site of the ancient Eordea; others, that Antipatria stood upon its site. B. is the cap. of a fertile but ill-cultivated region. It was recently governed by Muchtar Pasha, son of the celebrated Ali Pasha of Joanini.—Hughes.—Pocqueville.—Hobhouse. - Holland.

BERATINO, or ERGENT, a river of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Albania, which rises in the Grammos mountains, in the apex of the triangle formed by the Imolika range with Mount Pindus, here called Mount Grammos; runs along the northern side of Mount Tomoros; passes Dubrin and Berat, and thence flows W to the Adriatic, which it enters 25 m. below the latter town, and after an estimated course of 72 m. This river bears a variety of names, being also known as the Chervesta, the Tuberathi, and the Samana.
BERATSHAUSEN, or BERAZHAUSEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Regen, or the Upper Pfalz, and pres. of Hemau, on the r. bank of the Schwarze Laaber, 14 m. WNW of Ratisbon. Pop. 790.
BERAU, a village of Baden, in the circle of Constanz, SW of Donaueschingen. Pop. 383.

BERAUN, a central county of Bohemia, watered by the Beraun or Beraunka, a tributary of the Moldau. It is a fertile region, and finely diversified with hills and valleys. Its superficial area is 53 German, or 1,125 English sq. m.; and its pop. in 1832 amounted to 175,000, chiefly speaking the Bohemian language. Wood, grain, hops, and wine, form the chief produce of the soil. Silver, iron, lead, and quicksilver, are mined. The manufactures consist of porcelain, earthenware, cottons, linens, paper, and potash.-The cap., of the same name, called Slawoszow in Bohemian, is situated at the confluence of the B., and the Litawka, in N lat. 49° 58'. Pop. 2,200.—The river B. rises in the Bohemian forest, in the circle of Pilsen, and flows into the Moldau near Konigsaal.
BERBEGAL, a small town of Spain, in Aragon, 25 m. SE of Huesca, and 7 m. SW of Balbastro.
BERBENNO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in 7 m. NE of Morbegno. Pop. 2,015.

BERBERA. See BARBURRA. BERBERS-or more properly, in the plural form, Berabera people thinly scattered over nearly the whole of Northern Africa, and considered by many ethnologists to be the most ancient inhabitants of the region extending from the shores of the Atlantic to the confines of Egypt, over the different branches of the Atlas, and southward to the skirts of the Sahara. They are entirely distinct in person, language, and manners, both from the Moors and the Negroes; and while Langles and Marsden suppose them to be the descendants of the Carthaginians, Hereen and Hodgson are of opinion that they are descended from the people who inhabited Northern Africa previously to the foundation of Carthage by the Phœnicians, and who were known to the ancients by the names of Libyans and Numidians. They are also known by the names of Kabyles and Tuaricks, but which, according to Hodgson, are merely words signifying 'tribes.' The Tuaricks and Mezzabies are white, while other tribes inhabiting the same region, and speaking like them the Berber language, are black, with woolly hair, and Negro features. This fact may be accounted for by supposing that the white tribes are Arabs, who came into the country, perhaps in very ancient times, and have adopted the language of the natives. Or, it may be, that the white tribes are the representatives of the original inhabitants, while the black have emigrated from the Negro regions in the South. Future research must determine whether the ancient inhabitants of Northern Africa, before the period of Egyptian and Carthaginian occupation, were white or black. "There is every reason to believe," says Captain Washington, in an article in the 1st. vol. of the Geographical Journal, "that the Bereber is the native idiom of all Northern Africa: it is the language of the Mozabres, the Wadregans, the Wurgelans, the Tuaryks; and Berber words may be traced on the confines of Egypt and Abyssinia." The etymology of the word Berber cannot now perhaps be ascertained. Gibbon asserts that it is of Greek origin, and a corruption of the term Barbaroi; but it is not improbable that a Dutch colony, it was confined within the narrow boundaries of Abary creek on the W, by which it Surinam surrendered to the British in the year 1799, a negociation was entered into, by which the land between the Devil's creek and the Corentyn river BER was taken from Surinam and added to B., thus enlarging its territory by nearly a breadth of 45 m. With this addition, B. extends from Abary creek on the W, to the Corentyn on the E, along the sea coast about 150 m. The district stretches along the banks of the river of the same name. The plantations, on both sides of the river, extend nearly 100 m. from its mouth, and about 50 m. along the coast, which is low and flat. A carriage-road, 60 ft. broad, with parapets on each side, has been formed along the shore between the Demerara and the Corentyn to Demerara. This colony has only two rivers,—the Berbice and the Canje. The Berbice runs through it from S to N, and discharges itself into the Atlantic, 57 m. to the E of the Demerara. Without the entrance of this river is a bar of sand, over which at high tide there is seldom more than 16 ft. of water; but within the bar there is sufficient depth, and the river is navigable by ships of burden for about 50 m. from its mouth. Rather, however, than cross the bar, vessels generally prefer anchoring off the port of Demerara. The Canje is a narrow but deep stream, running at first nearly from S to N, but afterwards diverging to the E, till it joins the Berbice about 5 m. from its mouth. It is navigable for colony-schooners to about 40 m. from its mouth, at which point falls and cataracts occur. The population of this district, in 1811, was rethe prov. of Sondrio, near the N bank of the Adda, turned at 25,959, of whom 25,169 were Negroes. According to returns transmitted in 1833 the pop. was 21,541, of whom 19,541 were slaves, 1,651 free people of colour, and only 570 whites. Pauperism does not appear to exist in B. There is no labourer who cannot earn two guilders or 2s. 9d. per day by 7 hours' labour. The whole district is under the ecclesiastical care of the rector of New Amsterdam, and in the diocese of the bishop of Barbadoes. The thermometer seldom indicates a higher temp. than 91° in this colony; and its lowest range is about 75°.—The forests are extensive. The most remarkable object in the vegetable kingdom is a gigantic water lily, with leaves 6 ft., and flowers 15 in. in diam., which has received the name of Victoria Regia. The principal productions of the colony are sugar, coffee, tobacco, cocoa, cotton, and arnotto. Cotton thrives best on the coast-estates. In 1833 B. produced, in addition to the provisions required for home consumption, sugar 11,858,006 lbs.; coffee, 1,871,852 lbs.; cotton, 416,731 lbs.; molasses, 458,007 gallons; and rum, 339,398 gallons. In 1809 the colony exported 17,665 cwt. of cocoa; and 1,874,195 lbs. of cotton. In 1823, 74,221 gallons of rum, 56,000 cwt. sugar, and 18,537 lbs. of coffee, were imported into Great Britain from B. The following table exhibits the progress of the growth of sugar in this colony On the 31st of December, 1846, there were 47 sugar and coffee estates in cultivation in B. The revenue of the colony is raised by a capitation tax on the white and black inhabitants, an excise on every 50 pounds of sugar made, a weighage-toll of about two per cent. on all exports and imports, and a tonnage-duty of 3 florins per last on the burden of ships. The estimated value of its exports to the United Kingdom, in 1812, according to Colquhoun, was £306,490; and to all other parts, £28,971; of its imports from the United Kingdom, £93,468; and from all other parts, £35,149. In 1832 the imports amounted to £86,815, consisting principally of grain, fish, and lumber, from our North American colonies, and plantation stores from this country. The exports amounted to £332,933. The number of vessels that entered in 1832 was 289; the number that cleared out 312. Of both these numbers 28 were from Britain. In 1836, the imports amounted to £149,738; and the exports to £499,042. In the latter the article coffee amounted to £123,697, and sugar to £325,379. The exports in 1843 amounted to £195,459, of which the largest item was sugar, valued at £137,727. The other leading articles were molasses, rum, and coffee. The imports in the same year amounted to £69,219. In 1838, 163 vessels, of a total tonnage of 21,888 tons, arrived at the port of B.; in 1842, 103 vessels, tonnage 14,058 tons; and in 1846, 170 vessels, tonnage 20,002 tons. The only cap. of this colony is Fort Nassau or New Amsterdam. New Amsterdam, although planned by the Dutch, has been almost entirely built since the colony came into the possession of Britain, and is chiefly inhabited by British settlers. It is on a point of land on the E shore of the Berbice, and S side of the Canje, about a mile from the sea. Old Amsterdam can now be traced only in a few mounds and brick pavements. Hope Town, near Fort Wellington, is a large village of about 300 wattled, clayed, and thatched cottages, with a pop. of about 1,500; and Ithaca is somewhat larger. History.] The colony of B. was founded in 1626, by Van Peer of Flushing, who sent out several ships to trade with the Indians; and it flourished to such a degree, that the French, in an early descent upon the coast, were able to extort a contribution of 20,000 florins. In 1678, the settlement was granted, as an hereditary fief, to the family of Van Peer. On another attack being made upon the colony, by a flotilla of French privateers in 1712, a contribution of 300,000 florins was paid for the safety of the settlement. This sum was discharged by the house of Van Hoorn and Co., who received in return, from the family of Van Peer, three-fourths of the settlement. In 1720, the proprietors succeeded in raising a loan in shares for the cultivation of sugar. They were also permitted, by the Dutch East India company, to import 240 Negroes annually from Africa, at the rate of 165 florins a head; and the company became bound to furnish them with a greater number, if it were necessary, at the rate of 250 florins each. They were also allowed to dispose of their lands and slaves, and to levy a duty of 300 florins upon every vessel trading with the colony. By these means, the cultivation of sugar, cocoa, and indigo, was greatly extended; many fruitless searches were also made after mines. A fort was early built at the confluence of the Canje and Berbice rivers; but the dampness and marshiness of the coast induced the early settlers to build a town and fortress, called Zealandica, 50 m. up the river. The increase of the colony, however, and the difficulty of navigating the river, subsequently led them to build the town of New Amsterdam on the S side of the river Canje. The W banks of the river were first cultivated; but, in 1799, the country, as far to the E as the Devil's creek, was rapidly cleared of its wood, and became an extensive cotton-plantation. In 1796, B. capitulated to the English, who reinstated in office the former governor Van Batenburg. At the peace of Amiens, B., along with Demerara, was restored to the Dutch government; but it was again surrendered to the British under General Grinfield and Sir Samuel Hood, in September 1803, since which time it has continued in our possession, having been finally ceded in 1814. In 1831, Demerara, Essequibo, and Berbice, were united under one government called British Guayana.

BERBICE, a river of South America, in British Guayana, which rises in about 3° 50' N lat., and 57° 50' W long., and enters the Atlantic ocean in N lat. 6° 24'. Its stream at the mouth is 1\frac{1}{2} m. broad, and it is divided into two arms by a low bushy island, called Crab island. A bar of sand, 5 m. without the BER river, running from E to W, prevents vessels drawing more than 14 ft. water from entering; on which account vessels usually make the port of Demerara their anchorage, whence the supplies from the mother-country are transported, and the produce of the colony is returned by the same channel. The B., though broad, is shallow. The branch on the E side of Crab island does not exceed 15 or 16 ft. in depth; so that it is only navigable for moderate sized vessels, which can ascend as far as Fort Nassau about 50 m. by the river's course, from its mouth. The territory upon its shores lies low, and is covered with groves. The confluence of the Canjee takes place about 5 m. from the entrance. The river is navigable to 165 m. from its mouth for flat-bottomed canoes drawing 2 ft. water.
BERBIR. See GRADISKA.
BERCETTO, a cant. and town in the duchy and 25 m. SW of Parma, on the Bajanza. Pop. 750.
BERCHAD, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Podolsk, in the district and 15 m. NNE of Olgopol, and 28 m. SE of Bratslav, on the Beradka. Pop. 1,500.
BERCHEL, a village of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 39 m. SE of Grenada, on the Alpujarras. In the environs are some mineral springs.

BERCHEM, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 1 m. S of Antwerp, of which it forms a suburb. Pop. 2,729.—Also a commune and town of East Flanders, arrond. and 5 m. SW of Oudenarde, on the r. bank of the Schelde. It contains 25 mills, and 18 manufactories of chicory-coffee. Pop. 2,342.
BERCHIER, or BERCHER, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, in the district and 7 m. from Moudon. Pop. 332.

BERCHING, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Regen, in the pres. and 6 m. N of Bielengries, and 25 m. WNW of Ratisbon, on the Sulz. It contains upwards of 100 houses, and has a pop. of 1,350. Its commerce, which is active, consists chiefly in wine and fruit.
BERCHIS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of St. Gall, near the N bank of the Seez, 3 m. SE of Wallenstadt.

BERCHTESGADEN, a market-town in the Salzburg Alps, in Bavaria, with 1,424 inhabitants; famous for the salt-mines in its neighbourhood, the salt-works of Frauenreith, and the aqueducts which conduct the salt water to the works called Reichenhall. The rock-salt does not appear here in large solid masses, but in small pieces mixed with clay. Fresh water is let into the mines, and, having been saturated with salt, is carried into large reservoirs, from which, at the works of Frauenreith, 130,000 cwt. of salt are annually obtained. A large part of the saturated water is also conducted to Reichenhall. At this place a salt-spring was discovered in 1613, but, on account of a deficiency in wood required in the preparation of the salt, the water was conveyed, by means of an aqueduct, to Traunstein, 20 m. distant. Another aqueduct, 35 m. long, from Reichenhall to Rosenheim, was completed in 1809; and, in 1817, these were again brought into communication with B. The first machine, which raises the brine coming from B. 50 ft. high, is near this place. From hence it runs in pipes 3,500 ft. with a fall of 17 ft. only, into the second reservoir. A hydraulic machine, invented by Von Reichenbach, here lifts the salt water 311 ft. high, in iron pipes 934 ft. long. The water then runs in pipes 7,480 ft., with 37 ft. fall, to a valley over which it is led in iron pipes 1,225 ft. long, and, after running 12,073 ft. farther, falls into the third reservoir. Here is a second hydraulic machine, which lifts the water to a perpendicular height of 1,218 ft., in pipes 3,506 ft. long; and hence it flows, in pipes 73,000 ft. long, to Reichenhall. The pipes running from B. to Reichenhall amount to 104,140 ft. From Reichenhall to Siegsdorf there is but one aqueduct for the salt water intended for Traunstein and Rosenheim, 94,800 ft. long. From Siegsdorf to Traunstein the brine flows without an aqueduct. At Traunstein, 140,000 cwt. are annually produced. The other part of the brine flows in pipes, 78,000 ft. long, to Rosenheim, which produces annually 180,000 cwt. of salt. The water required to work the numerous machines is brought from places many of which are from 16,000 to 19,000 ft. distant.

BERCHTOLDSDORF, or Petersdorf, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, in the circle and 6 m. SSW of Vienna. It has mineral springs and baths. The environs afford considerable quantities of wine. Pop. 1,995.
BERCIZOMA. See Bergen-op-Zoom.
BERCK-SUR-MER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. and 10 m. from Montreuil, on the coast. It has a small port, and in 1840 possessed 74 fishing and coasting vessels. Pop. 1,649.
BERCLAU, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and com. of Billy-Berclau. Pop. 1,035.

BERCY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Charenton-le-Pont, on the r. bank of the Seine, and to the SE of Paris, of which it forms a suburb. It comprises the Petit-Bercy,-the port of B., -the Rapee, -Grande-Pinte, -and the valley of Fecamp, and has a fine castle. It possesses manufactories of vinegar and vitriol, sugar-refineries, distilleries, chemical-works, calico-printing mills, and tileworks; and is the great entrepot of wine, brandy, oil, and vinegar, destined for the market of Paris, &c. Pop. in 1831, 3,939.

BERCZELL, a village of Hungary, in the com. of Szabolcs, 30 m. NNE of Pest. It stands in the midst of salt-marshes; and the refining of the salt produced by their evaporation forms the chief employment of the inhabitants.
BERD (WADY-EL), a village of Nubia, on the caravan route between Essuan and Berber, 30 m. NNW of Tarfawy.

BERDA, a river of Russia in Europe, which takes its rise near the fort of Aleksievkaia, runs along the frontiers of the gov. of Ekaterinoslav and Taurida, and after a course, in a general direction from N to S, of 63 m., falls into the sea of Azov 6 m. S of Petrovskaia.
BERDA, or Berdaa, a town of Russian Persia, in the gov. of Georgia, near the l. bank of the Terter, 55 m. SE of Elizabetpol, and 120 m. E of Erivan. It was taken by Tamerlane in 1386.
BERDA NOVA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Servia, and sanjak of Kruchovatz, on the l. bank of the Morava, 30 m. NNW of Vrana, and 40 m. SSW of Nissa.
BERDE. See Berda, Persia.
BERDE (NAHR-EL), a river of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, which runs in a general direction ENE, and unites with the Barrada 8 m E of the town of Damascus.
BERDEBEK, a town in the Kirghiz-Kassuck steppe, to the SE of the mouth of the Sir-Deria.
BERDIA, a town of Spain, in Galicia, on the Tambre, 5 m. N of Santiago.

BERDIANSK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Taurida, at the mouth of the Berda, on the sea of Azov, 250 m. NE of Simferopol. Its port is one of the best on the sea of Azov, and Mariapol is being deserted for it. Pop. in 1840, 1,258. Its chief export is wheat.—Also a cape on the coast of the sea of Azov, 211 m. WSW of Bielosarai.
BERDIN, or Berdon, a parish in Essex, 5 m. BER NW of Stansted-Mountfitchet. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 391.
BERDISTAN (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of the Persian gulf, from which a range of hills called Halelah runs N and S to below Bushire.

BERDITCHEF, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Volhynia, in the district and 20 m. S of Jitomir. It is ill-built, but possesses several churches, and a Carmelite convent containing a celebrated image of the Virgin, on which a crown of gold was conferred by Pope Benedict in 1753. B. has long been celebrated for its fairs, four of which are held annually, and the trade of which consists in grain, wine, honey, wax, leather, and horses and cattle, amounting in yearly value to the sum of £800,000. At the principal horse-fair, there is sometimes a show of 40,000 horses; and the seigneurs of the country attend it with their families, and remain encamped in the neighbourhood of the town for three weeks. The pop., consisting chiefly of Jews, is estimated at 20,000.
BERDNIK, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Bosnia, in the sanj. and 70 m. NE of Trawnik, and 20 m. NW of Zvornik, on a tributary to the Save.
BERDO, a river of Norway, in the gov. of Finmark, which unites with the Mals river, and falls into the North sea in E long. 18° 40'.
BERE, or BEAR, an island in Bantry bay, co. Cork, 4 m. E of Blackhall-head. It is 5 m. in length, by 1½ to 2 m. in breadth.
BEREALSTON. See BEER-ALSTON.

BERDURANIS, a general name given to the tribes which inhabit the NE part of Afghanistan, between the Hindukush, the Indus, the Salt range, and the range of Soliman. They consist of Eusufzais, Turkolanis, Otmankhail, and Khyberees. They are divided into numerous little societies, and are all of agricultural habits.—Elphingstone.
BERE CHURCH, or West Domland, a parish in Essex, 2 m. S by W of Colchester. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 146.
BERE FERRIS. See BEER-FERRIS.
BERE HACKET. See BEER-HACKET.

BEREHAVEN, a spacious natural harbour in co. Cork, formed between Bere island and the mainland. Its quay is contiguous to the v. of Castleton. In 1835 the exports from this place amounted in value to £77,360, of which the chief items were copper-ore and butter.

BERE REGIS, a parish and market-town in Dorsetshire, 7 m. NW of Wareham. Area of p. 8,150 acres. Pop. 1,684. The town suffered severely from fire in 1634, 1788, and 1817. Dr. Stukeley supposes it to occupy the site of the Roman Ibernium.

BERECZK, a town of Transylvania, in the jurisdiction of Haromszek, in the Military Frontier, 60 m. E of Schosburg, at the entrance of the gorge or pass of Ojtosz into Moldavia. It is defended by a fort. Gypsum is found in the vicinity.
BERED, a town of Syria, in the pash. and 10 m. N of Tripoli, at the mouth of the river Nahr-el-Bered.

BEREGH, a comitat or administrative province of Hungary; bounded on the N by Austrian Galicia, from which it is separated by the Carpathian chain; on the E by the comitat of Marmaros; on the S by the comitats of Ugotsa and Szathmar; and on the W by those of Szabolts and Unghvar; comprising a superficial area of 1,434 sq. m. It is intersected on the N and E by mountain-ridges remarkable for their diversity of structure and outline. The entire chain which forms the northern frontier consists of coal sandstone, intermingled with more ancient calcareous formations; while in the adjacent plain are elevations of igneous origin, varying in extent, and sometimes isolated. The loftiest summits are the Brsava or Polonina, and the Borlo, both of which are of calcareous formation. The principal rivers are the Latorcza, Borzcya, and Theiss, which latter skirts the SW frontier. About 210,000 jochs are under cultivation, and 19,000 consist of meadowland; the remainder is to a great extent covered with forests. The chief productions of the soil are grain, hemp, lint, fruits, wine. The rearing of cattle, pigs, and bees forms an important branch of rural industry. Game and fish are abundant. There are some saline springs near the frontier towards Marmaros; and several chalybeate springs occur. Iron is found in the N and E districts, and gold mines are said to have existed in the mountain of Beregh-Szasz. Alum is extensively manufactured in various localities. The prov. is divided into the 4 jaras, or circles, of Munkacs, Kaszonye, Fel-Videk, and Tisza-Hat; and comprises 9 towns, of which the principal is Beregh- Szasz, and 258 villages. Its contribution to the military treasury amounts to 27,400 florins. The pop. in 1837 was 138,972, of whom one-third were Hungarians, and the remainder Slaves. Of this number, 60,000 belong to the Catholic church; 28,000 profess the reformed faith; and 6,000 are Jews. The office of governor of the com. of B. is hereditary in the family of the counts of Schönbron.—The town of Beregh, in the circle of Fel-Videk, near the marsh of Szernye, 6 m. ENE of Beregh-Szasz, contains the ruins of the ancient castle from which the prov. takes its name.

BEREGH-SZASZ, a town of Hungary, the cap. of the above prov. of Beregh. It is situated on the Borzova, 16 m. SSW of Munkacs. It contains 3 churches, a Catholic, a Reformed, and a United Greek. Pop. 4,500.
BEREGUARDO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 8 m. NW of Pavia, near the junction of the canal of the same name with the river Ticino. Pop. 480.
BEREKETLI, a town of Turkey, in Macedonia, in the sanjak of Gallipoli, NE of Kavala.
BEREKI. See BERGEIK.
BERENG. See BURENG.

BERENICE, an ancient port on the W side of the Red sea, in N lat. 23° 56', E long. 35° 34', at the bottom of a bay, the N side of which is formed by Ras Benas, which is 19 m. E by S from B. The ruins of the ancient city of B. are yet traceable [Belzoni] on the N shore of a small lagune; which seems to have served the purpose of an inner harbour, though its entrance is now choked with sand, and which extends for some space inland. [Wellsted.] The ancient city, built or restored by Ptolemy Philadelphus, was the great mart of commerce with India; and the goods received at B. were transported to Koptos, a city 3 m. distant from the Nile, but communicating with the river by a canal, and then carried down the river to Alexandria. [Robertson.] Pliny describes the camel-track followed by the caravans in crossing the Desert between Koptos and B., and says the whole occupied 12 days, which gives about 21 m. per diem for caravan-travelling. There were 10 hydreumata or watering-places on the route. —Belzoni's Researches.—Wellsted's Arabia.
BERENS, a river in British N America, rising in Deer lake, and flowing into Lake Winnipeg on the E side. Its head-streams approach those of Severn river, which flows into the W side of Hudson's bay.
BERENS (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of Boothia Felix, in N lat. 69° 4′ 12", W long. 90° 35'. It is formed entirely of granite partially covered with moss. The coast on both sides trends NW.—Rae.
BERENS' ISLES, a group in the Polar sea, in the Duke of York's archipelago, near the mouth of the Coppermine river, and intersected by the meridian of 115° W .- Franklin. BER
BERENTHAL. See BEHRENTHAL.
BERENY (Jasz), a town of Hungary, in the district of Jazygie, 30 m. E of Buda, on the Zagyva. Pop. 15,529.
BERESANSKOI, a liman or gulf on the Kherson coast of the Black sea. It extends about 40 versts inland, and is about 2 versts broad near Oczakof. Its entrance is choked with sands.
BERESHKI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Volhynia, on the Slutsh, NE of Bereshniza.
BERESHNIZA, a town of Russia, in Volhynia, on the Görun, NE of Luzk.

BERESINA, or BEREZINA, a river of Russian Lithuania, which rises in two main sources, one in the prov. of Minsk, and the other in the prov. of Wilna; traverses the gov. of Minsk, skirting the districts of Vileika and Borrizoff in a course from NW to SE; and falls into the Dnieper a little to the N of Reshitza, in the prov. of Mohilev, after a course of about 275 m., throughout the greater part of which it is navigable. Its affluents are the Plissa, the Swislocz, the Sergutsch, and the Ola. It is united by the Beresina or Lepel canal with the river Düna, by means of the Sergutsch river, the lakes Plavia, Bereshta, and Beloje, and the river Ulla, which taken together present a line of navigation 65 m. in length. The B. has been rendered historically famous by the disastrous passage which the French army, in their "horrible deroute," made across it on the 26th and 27th November, 1812. Admiral Tschitschakoff, with the Moldavian army, had forced his way from the south to join the main army, which, after Borizoff had been retaken, was to assist the army led by Witgenstein from the Dwina, and, in this manner, cut off Napoleon from the Vistula. Napoleon was, therefore, obliged to make the greatest efforts, in the face of immense difficulties occasioned by the nature of the country, the climate, and the critical situation of his troops, to reach Minsk, or, at least, the B., so as to pass it earlier than the Russians. To effect this it was necessary to sacrifice a great part of the baggage and artillery on the 25th of Nov. After the advanced guard of the Moldavian army had been repelled by Oudinot, and the bridge at Borizoff burnt by them, early in the morning of Nov. 26th, two bridges were built by the retreating army near Sembin, about 2 m. above Borizoff,-an undertaking the more difficult on account of both banks of the river being bordered by extensive morasses, covered, like the river itself, with ice not sufficiently strong to afford passage to the army, while other passes were already threatened by the Russians. Scarcely had a few corps effected their passage, when the greater part of the army, unarmed and in confusion, rushed in crowds upon the new bridges: discipline had long before disappeared, and the confusion increased with every minute. Those who could not hope to escape over the bridges sought their safety on the floating ice of the B., where most of them perished, while others were pushed into the river by their comrades. In this fatal retreat, the duke of Reggio (Oudinot) led the advanced guard, with the Poles under Dombrowsky in front; the rearguard was formed by the corps of the duke of Belluno. On the 27th of Nov., at noon, the dear-bought end was gained, and the army, leaving the road to Minsk, took that of Wilna to Warsaw, with the hope of providing for their necessities in Wilna. Besides the multitudes who were obliged to remain beyond the B., the division of Partouneaux, which formed the rear-guard, and was intrusted with the charge of burning the bridges in its rear, fell into the hands of the enemy.
BERESINO, or Beresna, a small town of Russia, in the gov. of Minsk, in the district of Borrizoff, on the l. bank of the Beresina, 44 m. NE of Minsk.
BERETTYO, a river of Hungary, an affluent of the Koros, which it joins near Mezo-Tur.
BERETTYO UJFALU, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Bihar, 21 m. S of Debretzin.
BEREZEK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Simbirsk, on the Sura river, 70 m. NE of Penza.
BEREZINA. See BERESINA.
BEREZNA, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Unghvar, 50 m. E of Kaschau.
BEREZINSKOI, a town of Siberia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the N side of the river Irtish, 45 m. E of Tobolsk.

BEREZOFSKY, a village of Siberia, 12 m. from Ekaterinsburg. There are extensive gold mines here in which about 6,000 hands are employed. The produce in 1820 was in the proportion of 21s. worth of gold for every 4,000 lbs. of earth.

BEREZOV, a town of Siberia, on the river Soswa, 18 m. from its confluence with the Obi, 420 m. N of Tobolsk, in N lat. 64°. B. provides Tobolsk, and other places on the Irtish, with dried fish in summer, and frozen fish during winter. It has also an active trade in furs. This is the most northern place in Siberia in which the horse can exist in health and vigour. Beyond B. the reindeer becomes the substitute for the horse. The ground freezes to the depth of 5 ft., yet barley and rye are cultivated with success. It was here that the three favourites of Peter the Great, Osterman, Dolgorukof, and Menschikof, ended their days in exile.
BEREZOVOI, a fortress of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, on the river Uvelka, 240 m. E of Upha, and 68 m. SE of Tcheliabinsk.

BERG, a duchy of Germany, in the old circle of Westphalia, bounded on the N by the duchy of Cleves; on the E by the county of Mark, and the duchy of Westphalia; on the S by the Westerwald; and on the W by the Rhine. It formerly belonged to the elector of Bavaria, but has been included, since 1815, in the grand duchy of the Lower Rhine which belongs to Prussia. In 1802 it contained an area of 1,134 sq. m., with 295,000 inhabitants. Towards the Rhine the country is level and fruitful; but in the eastern parts it abounds in woods and mountains, and is deficient in the supply of corn for its numerous population. There are mines of coal, iron, copper, lead, and quicksilver; and the principal objects of attention are its manufactures of iron, steel, linen, woollen, cotton, and silk. Its chief towns are Cologne and Dusseldorf.—The duchy of B. continued in the possession of the electors of Bavaria from 1666 until 1806, when it was ceded to France, and bestowed by Napoleon on his brother-in-law Murat, under the title of the grand duchy of B. There was at the same time added to it part of Cleves, the counties of Homburg, Bentheim-Steinfurt, Hortsmar, Nassau- Dietz, Dillenburg, Hadamar, and a number of lordships and scattered bailiwicks and towns. On Murat's receiving the kingdom of Naples, Bonaparte named his nephew, Napoleon Louis, eldest son of the king of Holland, hereditary grand duke of B., under the condition that the country should be under the immediate management of the French government, until the young prince should be of age. There was at the same time annexed to it the Prussian part of Munster, and the county of Mark; and the whole was divided into the dep. of the Rhine, the Ems, the Roer, and the Sieg, having a pop. of 878,000 on 6,908 sq. m. At the congress of Vienna, in 1815, the whole was given to the king of Prussia, who incorporated the larger portion with the prov. of Dusseldorf, and consolidated the remainder with the prov. of Juliers, Cleves, and Berg.

BERG, a small town of Sweden, in Jamtland, on an arm of Lake Storsio, 24 m. SW of Ostersund .- Also a town of Sweden, in the province of Smaaland, 12 m. W of Wexio.—Also a village of Norway, 12 m. S of Frederickstadt.—Also a town of Wirtemberg, in the circle of the lake of Constance, near the confluence of a small stream of the same name with the Aach, 4 m. N of Ravensburg. Pop. 1,200.—Also a village of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, 3 m. from Neumarkt.-Also a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Prachin, SW of Pisek .- Also a town in Limburg, on the r. bank of the Maas, 4 m. NW of Sittard.

BERG (GREAT), a river of S Africa, which rises in the district of Drakenstein, in the Stellenbosch mountains, and falls into St. Helena bay, in S lat. 32° 50', after a course of about 150 m. in a prevailingly N by W, and then W direction. BER
